meta.title: 2.1 What a Runtime Actually Is
meta.description: Understand the three runtime layers — engine, standard library, and runtime APIs — and why the differences between Node.js, Deno, Bun, and browser environments determine which npm packages work, what system calls are permitted, and what Make's cold start time will be.

blocks.prompt:
When we say "code is running," what does that actually mean? What is the thing that executes JavaScript, and where does it live? Write down your current answer before reading.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module gives you a precise vocabulary for the three layers of any JavaScript runtime — engine, standard library, and runtime APIs — and shows why the runtime choice is an architectural commitment that determines which npm packages can run, what the cold start cost is, and which framework features are available. By the end, you'll be able to explain why the same JavaScript code works in Node.js but fails in Cloudflare Workers, and why that matters for every infrastructure decision Make makes.

A runtime is not just "where code runs" — it's the specific combination of engine, standard library, and APIs that determines which code can run at all; choosing the wrong runtime for a feature is an architectural error that cannot be patched later without migration.

The word "runtime" is used loosely, but it has three distinct layers worth separating:

**The Engine** is the lowest layer — the thing that parses JavaScript syntax and executes it. Google's V8 engine (the JavaScript engine used in Chrome and Node.js — it compiles JavaScript to machine code using JIT, just-in-time compilation, which means it starts by interpreting your code and compiles frequently-run sections to optimized native code on the fly) uses just-in-time compilation: it interprets your code, profiles hot paths, then compiles them to optimized machine code on the fly. Mozilla's SpiderMonkey (Firefox) does similar work. Apple's JavaScriptCore (Safari, Bun) is a third variant. These engines share the language spec (ECMAScript), but they have different performance profiles, different garbage collection strategies, and subtle behavioral differences at the edges.

**The Standard Library** is the set of built-in modules the engine ships with. These are things like `Array.prototype.map`, `JSON.parse`, `Promise` — pure language constructs that don't touch the operating system. Every compliant JavaScript engine provides these identically.

**The Runtime APIs** are where runtimes diverge. These are the capabilities the runtime adds on top of the engine: `setTimeout` and `fetch` in the browser; `fs`, `http`, `path`, `child_process` in Node.js. These aren't part of the JavaScript language spec — they're provided by the runtime environment and they determine what your code can actually do.

This distinction matters enormously. A function that calls `fetch()` works in a browser, in Node.js 18+, in Deno, and in Cloudflare Workers — but a function that calls `require('fs').readFileSync()` only works in Node.js. Same language, different runtime, different capabilities.

**Node.js vs. Deno vs. Bun** are all JavaScript runtimes targeting server-side execution, but they make different choices:

- **Node.js** (2009): CommonJS modules (a module system using `require()` syntax, predating the modern ES Module standard) by default, npm ecosystem, massive package ecosystem built over 15 years. The V8 engine. Designed before modern security thinking — it trusts everything by default.
- **Deno** (2018): ES Modules (ESM — the modern JavaScript module standard using `import`/`export` syntax, natively supported by browsers) by default, TypeScript natively, explicit permission flags (`--allow-read`, `--allow-net`), and a standard library that doesn't depend on npm. Security-first. Imports via URL, not package names.
- **Bun** (2022): Drop-in Node.js compatibility goal, JavaScriptCore engine (faster startup), built-in bundler (a tool that combines many JavaScript files into fewer files for efficient delivery) and test runner. Fastest cold starts in benchmarks. Still maturing.

**Browser runtime vs. server runtime** is the other key axis. A browser runtime is sandboxed (isolated — it cannot escape the browser tab) by design — it cannot access your filesystem, make arbitrary network requests (CORS, Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, blocks this), or spawn child processes. The browser gives you APIs for DOM manipulation, `localStorage`, `IndexedDB` (a browser-based database for storing structured data), `ServiceWorker` (a script that runs in the background, intercepting network requests), `WebAssembly` (WASM — a binary format that runs near-native speed in browsers), and `Canvas`. A server runtime like Node.js has none of the DOM APIs but can read and write files, open TCP connections (direct network socket connections), spawn processes, and access environment variables.

## How to Think About This

The runtime API layer is the architectural commitment you can't easily change later. The engine (V8) is almost never the constraint — it's portable and fast everywhere. The standard library is identical across compliant runtimes. The runtime APIs — `fs`, `child_process`, `fetch`, DOM — are what lock code to a specific environment. When an npm package calls `require('fs')`, it's not writing JavaScript; it's writing Node.js-specific code. That package will silently fail or noisily crash in any runtime that doesn't provide the `fs` API. This is why "which runtime" is one of the first questions in any infrastructure review.

**Why "which runtime" is a platform-defining decision for Make.** Figma Make generates code that has to run somewhere — and that somewhere shapes everything downstream. The runtime determines:

- Which npm packages work. Native modules (compiled C++ binaries like `bcrypt` or `sharp`) only work in Node.js environments, not in browser-sandboxed runtimes.
- Which framework features are available. Next.js Server Actions assume Node.js APIs. Remix's streaming requires a runtime that supports `ReadableStream`.
- What system calls are permitted. Can the generated code write files? Open ports? Fork processes?
- What the cold start time is. Node.js with a full dependency tree: 500ms-2s. A V8 isolate (an isolated JavaScript execution context with its own heap — used by Cloudflare Workers): near-zero.
- What the cost model is. Each running Node.js process consumes memory. Each V8 isolate is ~10KB overhead.

A PM who understands runtimes can evaluate infrastructure proposals, challenge engineering assumptions, and understand why "just use npm package X" might be impossible in the chosen execution environment.

---

blocks.question: A developer says: "I need to use `sharp` for image processing in Make." Your engineering lead says: "That won't work in WebContainers." Why not — and what does this tell you about where WebContainers can and can't be deployed in your infrastructure?
blocks.answer:
`sharp` is a native module — it includes compiled C++ code (libvips) that must run as a binary on the host OS. WebContainers execute JavaScript via WebAssembly in the browser, which cannot load native binaries. This tells you that any feature requiring native npm packages (image processing, password hashing with bcrypt, database drivers that use binary protocols) cannot run in a browser-based execution environment. You'd need a server-side runtime for those capabilities.*

---

blocks.title:
Open three browser tabs:
1. **Node.js REPL on Replit** — run `process.versions` and `require('fs').readdirSync('.')`
2. **A browser DevTools console** — try `process` (undefined) and `window.location` (works)
3. **StackBlitz** — create a blank Node.js project, try `import { readFileSync } from 'fs'`

Note what succeeds and fails in each environment. The failures are more informative than the successes.

---
blocks.solution: In the Node.js REPL on Replit, `process.versions` should return an object showing the Node.js version and its V8 engine version — proof you're in a server runtime. `require('fs').readdirSync('.')` should list directory contents, confirming filesystem access. In the browser DevTools console, `process` should be `undefined` — there is no Node.js process object in a browser environment. `window.location` works because the browser runtime provides the `window` global. In StackBlitz, the `import { readFileSync } from 'fs'` attempt depends on whether you're in a WebContainers environment: if you are, it may work because WebContainers emulates the Node.js `fs` module via a virtual filesystem. If something fails unexpectedly, that's the most valuable outcome — it reveals precisely where the runtime boundary sits. The point is not to memorize which things work, but to internalize that the same JavaScript code has different behavior depending entirely on which runtime is executing it.

blocks.prompt:
Without using the words "runtime" or "environment," explain to a designer why the code that Make generates for "generate an image thumbnail" might work in the preview but fail when deployed. What is actually different between those two places?

---

questions:
Think of a product or feature you shipped that had to work across multiple platforms — web, mobile, or different deployment targets. Where did you discover an assumption baked into the code that turned out to be platform-specific? How did you find out, and what did it cost to fix?

---

blocks.content:
A runtime is not just "where code runs" — it's the specific combination of engine, standard library, and APIs that determines which code can run at all; choosing the wrong runtime for a feature is an architectural error that cannot be patched later without migration.

---

blocks.prompt:
You now know what a runtime is and that different runtimes have different capabilities and costs. The most common server-side runtime deployment unit is a Docker container. But containers have properties that make them problematic for interactive preview environments. What do you predict those problems are?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: What are the three distinct layers that make up a JavaScript runtime?
options: Engine, standard library, and runtime APIs
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module defines a runtime as a combination of three layers: the engine (which executes JavaScript), the standard library (built-in language constructs like Array.map), and the runtime APIs (environment-specific capabilities like Node.js's fs or the browser's DOM). The runtime API layer is where runtimes diverge and why the same code behaves differently across environments.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice! Those three layers are the foundation for understanding why the same JavaScript behaves differently in Node.js, the browser, and Cloudflare Workers.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: A runtime has three layers: the engine (executes JS code), the standard library (built-in language constructs like Promise and Array.map), and the runtime APIs (environment-specific capabilities like fs or the DOM). The API layer is where runtimes diverge and why some code only runs in Node.js.

reviewQuestions.question: Which statement correctly describes why `require('fs').readFileSync()` fails in Cloudflare Workers?
options: Cloudflare Workers use an older version of Node.js that doesn't support fs
readFileSync is deprecated and replaced by async file APIs across all modern runtimes
fs is a Node.js runtime API — not part of the JavaScript language spec — and Cloudflare Workers deliberately exclude it to maintain their V8 Isolate API surface
reviewQuestions.explanation: The fs module is a Node.js runtime API, not part of the ECMAScript standard. Cloudflare Workers run JavaScript via V8 Isolates with a deliberately restricted API surface — no fs, no child_process, no arbitrary TCP — because those restrictions are what enable sub-millisecond cold starts. The same language, different runtime APIs.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — fs is a Node.js-specific API, not a language feature. This is the core runtime API distinction the module builds toward.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: readFileSync is not deprecated, and Cloudflare Workers run modern JavaScript. The issue is that fs is a Node.js runtime API — it was never part of the JavaScript language spec — and Cloudflare Workers intentionally omit it to maintain their lightweight V8 Isolate model. Same language, different runtime APIs.

reviewQuestions.question: Deno's security model is described as 'security-first.' What is the specific mechanism that makes this true?
options: Deno requires explicit permission flags like --allow-read and --allow-net for any system access — no access is granted by default
Deno encrypts all file I/O and network traffic by default, unlike Node.js
Deno runs each module in a separate V8 Isolate, preventing cross-module data access
reviewQuestions.explanation: Deno's security model is opt-in: code runs with zero system access by default, and each type of access (file reads, network, environment variables) must be explicitly granted via flags. Node.js trusts everything by default. This means a Deno script can't secretly exfiltrate data without the user granting network access.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Well done — the explicit permission flag system is Deno's defining security property and a meaningful architectural difference from Node.js.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Deno's security model is about explicit permissions, not encryption or Isolate-per-module. By default, a Deno script has no access to the filesystem, network, or environment variables. Each must be granted via flags like --allow-read or --allow-net. Node.js grants all of these by default.
